report_name: Phantom Squatting: Attackers Weaponize AI-Hallucinated Domains in Novel Supply Chain Attacks

attack_patterns.name: Discover LLM Hallucinations
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries systematically query Large Language Models (LLMs) to map the 'hallucination surface'—the set of phantom domains the model is likely to generate for a target brand or its software.

attack_patterns.name: Publish Hallucinated Entities
attack_patterns.description: Attackers preemptively register AI-hallucinated domains to create a malicious entity (website) that appears authoritative when recommended by an LLM.

attack_patterns.name: Acquire Infrastructure: Domains
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries register the phantom domains that LLMs are likely to hallucinate, creating a new attack surface.

attack_patterns.name: Stage Capabilities
attack_patterns.description: Attackers set up malicious infrastructure, such as phishing sites or data interception servers, on the newly registered phantom domains.

attack_patterns.name: Develop Capabilities
attack_patterns.description: Attackers use AI coding assistants to build their malicious tools, such as the Montana Empire phishing kit, demonstrating a full AI-driven attack cycle.

attack_patterns.name: Spearphishing Link
attack_patterns.description: The Large Language Model (LLM) itself acts as the delivery mechanism for a malicious link to a phantom domain, lending it an air of legitimacy that bypasses traditional phishing detection.

attack_patterns.name: Malicious Link
attack_patterns.description: An end-user (developer) or an automated system (CI/CD pipeline) clicks a malicious link or executes an HTTP request to a phantom domain provided by a trusted LLM.

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
attack_patterns.description: The Phantom Squatting attack vector targets the software supply chain by compromising developers and CI/CD pipelines through trusted, but hallucinating, LLMs.

malware.name: Montana Empire
malware.description: A phishing kit deployed on a phantom domain that was predicted by researchers. It is used to steal credentials and other sensitive information.

campaigns.name: Phantom Squatting
campaigns.description: A novel software supply chain attack vector where adversaries register AI-hallucinated domains to intercept traffic, deploy malware, and conduct phishing campaigns against developers and automated systems.

identities.name: Software Developers
identities.description: A primary target of Phantom Squatting attacks, as they interact with LLMs for coding assistance and may trust the hallucinated URLs provided.

identities.name: CI/CD Pipelines
identities.description: Automated systems targeted by Phantom Squatting, which may be configured to trust and connect to endpoints suggested by integrated LLMs, leading to secret and data exfiltration.

infrastructure.name: Phantom Squatting Domains
infrastructure.description: AI-hallucinated domains that are preemptively registered by attackers to host malicious content, intercept API calls, or serve as phishing sites.

indicators.name: Suspicious Outbound Connection from Developer Tool
indicators.pattern:
title: Suspicious Outbound Connection from Developer Tool
id: 2b8a1c4d-f3e2-4b9a-8c7d-1e6f0a5b4d3c
status: experimental
description: Detects developer tools like curl or wget making outbound HTTP(S) connections, which could indicate interaction with a phantom-squatted domain as described in the Unit 42 report on AI-hallucinated domains. This is a hunting rule and requires contextual analysis of the destination.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/phantom-squatting-hallucinated-web-domains/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-07-01
tags:
  - attack.t1204.001
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: linux
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith:
      - '/curl'
      - '/wget'
    CommandLine|contains:
      - 'http://'
      - 'https://'
  filter_internal:
    CommandLine|contains:
      - 'localhost'
      - '127.0.0.1'
  condition: selection and not filter_internal
indicators.description: Detects developer tools like curl or wget making outbound connections, which could indicate interaction with a phantom-squatted domain as described in the Unit 42 report. This is a hunting rule and requires context.

relationships.target_name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools

relationships.source_name: Suspicious Outbound Connection from Developer Tool
